title: Chapter 28: The Diary
content: The delivery lorry came at dusk, when the Loire had turned to hammered copper and the château's shadow stretched long across the gravel drive. Jane watched from the staircase window as two men in canvas gloves hauled boxes from the truck bed — her boxes, the rest of her life, shipped from the flat in Hammersmith that Helen had finally finished clearing out.

She had forgotten how much she owned. Cardboard boxes stamped FRAGILE and THIS SIDE UP, a steamer trunk she'd bought at a charity shop in Shepherd's Bush, two suitcases worn soft at the corners from years of Northern Line commuting. Smells drifted up the stairwell as the men carried everything past: dust from the flat's storage cupboards, the ghost of London rain soaked into cardboard, and beneath it all, something faintly chemical — the residue of a city that never quite washed clean.
Adèle appeared at the foot of the stairs, her phone abandoned mid-scroll. "That is everything from London?"

"Apparently." Jane descended, counting boxes as the men stacked them in the front hall. Twelve. Thirteen. Fourteen. And one more — smaller than the rest, wrapped in brown paper, no shipping label. She frowned.
The men left. The front door groaned shut. The château settled back into its own silence — that particular hush of old stone absorbing sound, as though the building itself were listening.

Jane carried the small box upstairs. It was lighter than she expected; the weight of it barely registered against her palm. In her room, she set it on the bed and peeled back the brown paper. Inside, nested in tissue paper the colour of old cream, was a leather diary.
She knew the handwriting before she opened it.

There, on the first page, in ink faded from black to the grey-brown of old blood: *M. Eyre — Personal Notes.* Her mother's hand. Neat, careful, architectural — each letter constructed with the precision of someone who drew technical schematics for a living and could not afford a stray line.

Jane sat on the edge of the bed. Her fingers trembled against the leather cover, which was soft and worn at the corners, the spine cracked from use. She pressed it to her nose and inhaled. Tobacco — her mother had smoked, a secret she thought Jane didn't know — and something else. Tea. The sharp tannin tang of builders' tea, strong enough to strip paint. For a moment, the smell was so vivid that Jane was seven again, sitting on the kitchen counter in their flat in Leeds, watching her mother frown over fabric swatches at the table, mug steaming beside her elbow.

Helen must have found it while packing. Helen, who knew that Jane would want it, who had wrapped it in tissue paper and sandwiched it between cookbooks so it wouldn't be crushed. Helen, who understood that some objects are not things but doors.
She did not open it until midnight.

The château had gone quiet — that particular quality of silence that descends on old houses after the staff have retired, when even the pipes stop their ticking and the only sound is the Loire moving against its banks, far below. Jane had changed into a cotton camisole and sat cross-legged on the bed with the lamp pulled close, its warm gold light making a small bright world on the duvet.

The diary was thin. Her mother had died when Jane was nine — cancer, fast and merciless, the kind that doesn't give you time to write a magnum opus. But the pages were dense, the handwriting small and economical, as though she'd known she was working against a limit.

The early entries were technical. Jane turned pages slowly, her fingertips brushing the impressions left by her mother's pen — the grooves in the paper like scars, like braille for a language she was only beginning to learn. *Warp-faced twill, 2/1 interlace. Tensile strength 320 MPa under standard conditions. Note: shrinkage exceeds stated tolerance at 40°C wash — flagged to procurement, no response.*

Her mother had been a textile engineer. Not a designer — a technician. Someone who understood how things were made, how they held together, where they failed. Jane had always known this in the abstract, but reading it brought the knowledge into her body. The same way she understood fabric now: not as surface, but as structure. Her mother had seen the architecture inside a weave the way Jane saw the architecture inside a silhouette. The same eye. The same impatience with anything that looked beautiful but couldn't bear weight.
She turned more pages. *Denier inconsistency in Lot 472. Fibre composition analysis shows 12% synthetic content where certification claims 0%. Have documented. Will escalate.*

The name of the company appeared in the margins: *Meridian Textiles Ltd.* Jane's breath caught. She knew that name. Not from her mother — from Edward's procurement files. Meridian had been absorbed into the Thornfield supply chain seven years ago. Its factories, its certifications, its contracts — all of it now operated under the Thornfield Group umbrella. The same suppliers. The same chemical treatments. The same organic certifications that Bertha's documents had already proven were fraudulent.
Twenty years ago. Her mother had been flagging the same fraud, in the same supply chain, two decades before Jane ever set foot in this château.

The technical notes continued, but something shifted in the handwriting around the middle of the diary. The letters tightened. The lines pressed harder into the page. *PM asked me to certify Lot 503 as organic. I refused. He said I was "not a team player." I said I was not a liar. Meeting ran 45 minutes. No resolution.*

*Not a team player.* The phrase landed in Jane's chest like a stone dropped into still water. She had heard those exact words before — in her own performance review at Thornfield, the one Edward had quietly suppressed. The same language. The same mechanism. A woman with expertise, standing in the way of profit, dismissed as difficult.
And then, on the next page, a new name appeared.

*B came to my desk today. She'd seen my report. She said she'd noticed the same discrepancies in her own work — the dyes, the finishing agents, the chemical residues that shouldn't exist in certified organic fabric. She's a designer. Young. Brilliant. She sees what I see: that the beauty is a lie, and the lie is killing people.*

Jane's hands had gone cold. She knew — with the gut-level certainty that comes from spending weeks tracing the same malignant thread through fabric and document and silence — who B was. The designer who had come before Bertha. The woman who had died. The accident that was never an accident.

She pressed on. The entries grew shorter, more urgent, the technical observations giving way to something rawer. *B filed a formal complaint with the board. I co-signed. Human Resources acknowledged receipt. No action taken. The testing irregularities continue. B is receiving pressure from E.R. to withdraw.*
Edward Rochester. Even the initials were enough.

*B was hospitalized last week. Stress, they said. She told me they've accessed her medical records. They're building a case that she's unstable. That her complaint is the product of her diagnosis, not her expertise. I told her they couldn't do that. She laughed. She said, "They already are."*

Jane could smell it now — not just the tobacco and tea from the diary's pages, but something else rising from the text itself. The familiar reek of institutional gaslighting. Of a woman's credibility being dismantled piece by piece, each element of her life weaponized against her. She had felt it herself, that slow constriction, the way Edward's charm could turn into a cage. Bertha had lived it. And before Bertha, B had lived it too.
The entry was dated a Thursday. The ink had smudged in places, as though her mother's hand had moved too fast for the words.

*B called me in tears. She said they were going to destroy her. She said no one would believe her because of her diagnosis. I told her to fight. She said she was too tired. I should have gone to her. I should have—*

The sentence stopped. The pen had dragged off the edge of the line, leaving a thin scoring mark where the nib bit into paper. Below it: nothing. A blank page. Then another. The emptiness of those pages was louder than any words — the silence of a woman who could not finish writing what she had failed to do.

A week later, the diary resumed. The handwriting had changed. Still precise — her mother had been too disciplined for sloppiness — but something essential had been removed from the strokes. They were flat. Mechanical. The handwriting of a woman writing because she had to, not because she believed it mattered.

*B is gone. They said it was an accident. It wasn't an accident. I know it wasn't. The same people who told her she was unstable, who accessed her medical records, who made her doubt her own mind — they are telling the world she fell. She did not fall. She was pushed. And I did nothing. I was safe in my flat with my certificates and my clean conscience and I did nothing, and she is dead, and the fraud continues, and no one will ever know because they have made sure that the only people who witnessed it are either dead or silent or—*
The entry ended there. The diary had no more pages.

Jane closed the diary. She held it in both hands, the leather warm now from her touch, and pressed it against her sternum. She could feel her own heartbeat through the cover — or maybe it was her mother's, preserved in the grain of the leather like a frequency that had never stopped broadcasting.
Her mother had tried.

She had done exactly what Jane was doing now: documented the fraud, reported it through official channels, co-signed a complaint with a colleague who believed the same things. And the system had swallowed them. B was dead. Her mother had retreated into silence and safety, carrying the guilt like a stone in her chest, and she had never spoken of it — not to Jane, not to anyone. The diary was the only confession, and it had been packed away in a flat in Hammersmith for nearly two decades, waiting.

The rage came slowly, not as fire but as pressure — the kind that builds in deep water, where the current is invisible from the surface but powerful enough to shape the ocean floor. Twenty years. The same company. The same fraud. The same man at the top. And three women — her mother, B, Bertha — each standing in the same spot, each fighting the same battle, each destroyed or silenced or driven into exile by the same machinery.
Jane set the diary on the pillow and reached for her phone. The screen glowed in the dark room. She typed:
*I need you to find everything you can about a designer who worked for Thornfield's predecessor company. Initial B. Died approximately 17 years ago. Called an accident. It wasn't.*
She sent it to Helen. The message delivered with a soft chime that sounded, in the silence of the château, like a bell rung at the bottom of a well.
Then she opened a new note and wrote, her thumbs steady:
*My mother tried. B tried. Bertha tried. I'm the fourth woman to stand in this exact spot. I refuse to be the fourth woman to fail.*
She stared at the words for a long time. Then she saved the note and set the phone aside.

The room was dark now except for the thin blade of moonlight that had crept across the floor. Jane picked up the diary again and held it against her chest, her mother's handwriting pressed to her heartbeat, her mother's fight folded into her own. The leather smelled of tobacco and tea and time — the musty sweetness of things preserved past their natural lifespan, kept alive by the stubborn insistence that someone, someday, might need to read them.
Someone had.

Through the floor, faint and rhythmic, she heard a sound she had come to recognize. The swift, precise whisper of scissor blades closing on fabric. Snick. Snick. Snick. Bertha, working late in the atelier below, cutting a pattern with the focused deliberation of a woman who understood that precision was its own form of justice.

Jane closed her eyes. In the dark, she could feel them — the women who had stood here before her. Her mother at her desk with her test reports and her mug of builder's tea. B in the meeting room, filing a complaint no one would hear. Bertha in the atelier, scissors in hand, transforming waste into witness. And now Jane, in this room that smelled of old leather and new resolve, holding a diary that proved what she had always suspected: the fraud was not an anomaly. It was an architecture. Built to last. Designed to destroy.
But architectures can be taken apart. You just need to find the load-bearing wall.

The scissors continued below, patient and precise. A woman cutting a pattern. A woman who had already survived what B could not, who had already endured what her mother had fled, who sat in a basement atelier at two in the morning with nothing but fabric and fury and the certainty that the truth, like a well-constructed seam, would hold.

The fight was generational. The system had destroyed women for decades — pushed them, silenced them, driven them to the edge and called the result an accident. But now, for the first time, the women were talking to each other. Across time, across silence, across the same damn fraud.
Her mother's diary lay open on the duvet like a door.
Jane walked through.
